THE LAW OF MOORE, FORMULATED BY INTEL CO-FOUNDER GORDY MOORE IN 1965, PREDICTS THAT THE NUMBER OF TRANSISTORS ON A MICROPROCESSOR WILL DOUBLE EVERY TWO YEARS. THIS TRENDS HAS DRIVEN THE RAPID ADVANCEMENT OF COMPUTING TECHNOLOGY FOR DECADES. HOWEVER, AS WE APPROACH THE PHYSICAL LIMITS OF SILICON BASED TECHNOLOGY, IT BECOMES INCREASINGLY DIFFICULT TO CONTINUE THIS EXPONENTIAL GROWTH. SCIENTISTS AND ENGINEERS ARE NOW EXPLORING ALTERNATIVE MATERIALS AND NEW COMPUTING PARADIGMS TO EXTEND THE LAW'S RELEVANCE.

*** 

WHILE THE LAW OF MOORE MAY NOT "STOP" IN A STRICT SENSE, ITS RATE OF PROGRESSION IS BECOMING MORE CHALLENGING TO MAINTAIN. MANY EXPERTS BELIEVE THAT THE LAW WILL NO LONGER HOLD TRUE BY THE MID-2020S, AS PHYSICAL CONSTRAINTS SUCH AS HEAT DISSIPATION AND QUANTUM EFFECTS BECOME MORE SIGNIFICANT. THIS DOES NOT MEAN THE END OF TECHNOLOGICAL PROGRESS, BUT RATHER A SHIFT TOWARD DIFFERENT APPROACHES TO INCREASING COMPUTING POWER. THE FUTURE OF COMPUTING MAY LIE IN NEW MATERIALS, NANOTECHNOLOGY, AND ADVANCED ARCHITECTURES RATHER THAN SIMPLE SCALING. COMPUTING POWER WILL CONTINUE TO INCREASE, BUT IN DIFFERENT WAYS.
